one.en: PARENTING – Unacceptable risk – Where the father is facing multiple serious criminal charges including sexual assault – Where the Court has previously…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Unacceptable risk – Where the father is facing multiple serious criminal charges including sexual assault – Where the Court has previously made findings that another child is at an unacceptable risk of harm in the care of the father and ordered for no time between the child and the father – Where the mother raises serious allegations of family violence and coercive and controlling behaviour – Where the Court found the father was the perpetrator of family violence – Where the father contended that the risk can be mitigated through supervised time – Where the mother and Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) contended that the risk cannot be mitigated and sought for the father to spend no time with the child – Where the Court is satisfied that the father poses an unacceptable risk even in a supervised setting – Orders made for the father to spend no time with the child. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where both parties sought an order by way of financial adjustment – Where the mother sought 70 per cent of the net asset pool – Where the father sought 65 per cent of the net asset pool – Where contributions were assessed as equal – Where the mother contended that the family violence she suffered affected her ability to make contributions – Where the Court is satisfied that this has been established – Where the mother is and will be the sole carer of the child and receives limited child support from the father – Where an adjustment in favour of the mother is appropriate – Where a just and equitable outcome is 60 per cent to the mother and 40 percent to the father.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

These are proceedings pursuant to Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) in relation to the parties’ daughter, X (“the child”), born 2021 and pursuant to Pt VIII of the Act by way of division of their property.
The mother’s application sought sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with her and spend no time with the father. The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”) supported the mother’s position.
The father sought orders as summarised in his Case Outline, as follows:
1. The father agrees to an order that vest sole decision making in the mother.
The father seeks to be informed of significant decisions made for the medical treatment or education of the child be provided to him within 7 days of those decisions being made.
That the father seeks to spend supervised time with the child, in an appropriate Child Support Facility once a fortnight paid for by the father for a minimum period of 2 hours.
That the father seeks that the paternal grandparents spend supervised time with them, in an appropriate Child Support Facility on the alternate fortnight for a minimum period of 2 hours. Cost to be paid for by the father.
In cross examination, the father expanded upon what was contemplated by paragraph two. As best I understood his evidence, he sought to be informed in relation to matters relating to the child’s development including her progress at school, her health, what she was eating, her favourite colour, and her general progress.
I advised the father’s lawyer at the commencement of the hearing that I did not intend to consider what was contemplated by the terms of paragraph four in circumstances where the time for the filing of amended relief had long passed and there was no affidavit evidence from the paternal grandparents.
For the reasons set out below, I intend to make orders as sought by the mother.
In relation to the financial proceedings, the parties were agreed as to the identity and value of the asset pool for division, except for the value of the jointly owned former matrimonial home. They remained apart in relation to both the assessment of contributions and any adjustments under s 79(5) of the Act.
In that respect, the mother contended that the contributions should be assessed in her favour at 60 per cent, with a further 10 per cent adjustment under s 79(5), resulting in a 70/30 percentage division in her favour. The father contended that contributions should be assessed in the mother’s favour at 30 per cent, with a 5 per cent adjustment under s 79(5), resulting in a 35/65 percentage division in the mother’s favour.
For the reasons set out below, I am not satisfied that the proposal of either party gives effect to a just and equitable outcome.

BACKGROUND

The mother was born in 1987 and is currently 38 years of age.
The father was born in 1988 and is currently 37 years of age.
The parties commenced cohabitation in May 2019, married in late 2019 and separated on a final basis on 17 October 2022.
The parties have one child together, X, who was born in 2021 and is currently four years of age.
The father has one child from a previous relationship, H (aged 10), who is not the subject of these proceedings. H resides with her mother and spends no time with the father pursuant to final parenting orders made by McGuire J on 22 December 2022.
The mother is a health professional and, until the birth of the child, worked regular casual shifts. Since the birth, she has only been able to work occasional daytime shifts due to her caregiving responsibilities. When she has worked, her mother has cared for the child.
The father is a tradesperson and owns his own business.
In mid-2018, the father received a compensation payout as a result of an accident. The specific amount is unclear; however, he deposed that he had approximately $260,000 in savings following the payout (father’s affidavit filed 22 March 2024 (“father’s affidavit”), paragraph 10).
In September 2018, the father utilised those funds to purchase vacant land at J Street, Town K (“[Town K] property”).
At the commencement of cohabitation, the parties lived between each other’s homes before moving into a renovated shipping container on the Town K property while renovations were being undertaken.
In late 2019, the parties purchased a property at E Street, Suburb F (“the former matrimonial home”) in their joint names. The property was purchased using both parties’ savings and a mortgage was taken out with L Bank. The parties are at issue as to how much they contributed to the purchase.
After purchasing the former matrimonial home, the father undertook renovations and improvements to the property.
In early 2020, the father sold the Town K property.
In May 2020, the parties paid off the mortgage over the former matrimonial home using cash from savings, accumulated income, the sale proceeds of the Town K property, funds from each party’s superannuation, and proceeds from the sale of some chattels.
Following separation, the mother vacated the former matrimonial home with the child and went to reside in her mother’s one-bedroom apartment for approximately five weeks. Due to the limited space and safety concerns, she says she and the child then moved to “alternative share-accommodation” for a brief period before securing a two-bedroom rental apartment with her mother. The mother says that they shared the rent and bills.
The mother remained in this rental accommodation until on or about September 2025 (mother’s affidavit filed 6 November 2025 (“mother’s affidavit”), paragraph 26), when her mother moved to New South Wales and she could no longer afford the rent. Since that time, the mother says that she been “essentially homeless” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 232), temporarily residing with a friend in a self-contained area beneath her house. She also purchased a van and has been travelling in that with the child for the past couple of months (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 233).
The father has continued to reside in the former matrimonial home following separation. The mother alleged that, post-separation, the father sub-let rooms or has tenanted the entire property and moved in with his parents living rent free (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 222 and 223), and that he has solely received the benefit of this rental income since separation.
The mother deposed to a tumultuous relationship. The father provided little to no evidence as to the dynamics of the relationship.
The mother contended the father perpetrated family violence against her. In her trial affidavit, the mother deposed to several instances of family violence including physical, sexual and emotional abuse in the presence of the child, as well as stalking and intimidation. The mother contended that the father regularly subjected her to verbal abuse, including swearing at her and calling her a “fucking cunt”, “fucking bitch”, “spoiled bitch” and telling her that she was “was too selfish to be a mother” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 70 and 97). She further contended that the father frequently contacted her to ascertain her whereabouts and activities, becoming angry if she was not at home (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 72).
The mother says that, following separation, the father would turn up at her mother’s home, where she and the child were residing, without notice and continued to do so despite requests that he cease this behaviour. She says that when the father discovered that she was no longer residing at that address, he incessantly telephoned and messaged her. The mother deposed that she was “genuinely fearful that [Mr Frost] would kill me post-separation” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 128).
For his part, the father provided a blanket denial that he was the perpetrator of family violence but did not respond to any of the specific allegations raised by the mother. The mother was not cross examined on any of the allegations.
The mother contended the father had a substance use disorder, consuming marijuana and alcohol to excess. The mother says that the father’s “level of aggression would increase when he consumed alcohol and marijuana. Initially he would relax and then he would become even more aggressive than what he already was without it” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 151).
The single expert, Dr Ratcliff, recorded in his report dated 17 July 2025 (“the Single Expert Report”) that:
The Father, [Mr Frost] displays behavioural and personality characteristics consistent with disorders resembling ... 2) Cannabis Use Disorder (according to DSM-5 TR) but not satisfying a sufficiency of clinical features to establish these diagnoses.

(Single Expert Report dated 17 July 2025, p.22)
This issue was not pursued in cross-examination, other than the father stating that he “didn’t realise there was such a thing”.
The single expert recorded the father’s behaviour in the following way:
His attitude to women appears to be that they should be sexually attractive, available and compliant. An ability to attract, to please and eventually to establish a sexual relationship with a suitable woman can be viewed as one face of the need to control. The more negative face of that would emerge later in reported controlling behaviour.

(Single Expert Report dated 17 July 2025, p.20–21)
In cross-examination, the father did not accept Dr Ratcliff’s conclusion that he had a sexual addiction and a need to control women. He further denied ever raping the mother.
Following separation, the father spent time with the child on eight occasions between October 2022 and February 2023. These occasions were supervised by the mother at a park. The mother described these visits as “ad hoc, irregular and short” and that the father “did not seem ... interested in spending time with [X]” and instead “would direct his attention to me and try to convince me to resume a relationship with him” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 41 and 42).
The mother contended that in February 2023 she ceased facilitating these visits as she was “uncomfortable with his pressure to recommence the relationship” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 43).
The mother subsequently contacted N Contact Centre and arranged for professionally supervised visits to commence. Between March 2023 and September 2023, the father spent time with the child for one hour each week under the supervision of N Contact Centre.
Since September 2023, the father has not spent any time with the child. The mother deposed that she was advised by N Contact Centre that, after six months, the service could only provide monthly visits. She contended that, following this advice, she proposed to the father that weekly visits continue using the supervision services of O Family Services at the father’s cost, but received no response.
In late 2023, a Police Family Violence Order was issued against the father by Tasmania Police.
In late 2024, the father was charged with several criminal offences of rape against the mother and his former partner.
On 5 January 2025, the mother received a message from the father’s most recent partner, Ms P, which read:
...I am very sorry to bother you on a Sunday but I am in hospital after escaping sexual assault rape and domestic violence from a 14 month relationship with [Mr Frost] and asking please if you have a good lawyer with good knowledge with these cases as I am also pregnant for the second time with his child and have no family or social supports...

(Exhibit 5)
On 11 February 2025, the mother received further messages from Ms P which read:
You did the right thing by stopping [Mr Frost] from seeing your daughter he is insane...He’s a very dangerous man and completely unhinged. He has no fear of the police he got pepper sprayed and even had their gun!...He mentioned in his rage filled rant from a private number that he’d “just take her I’m assuming your daughter and they’d never find us again.” so please be careful. Sorry if this is triggering but I felt you should know he’s completely lost touch with reality...

(Exhibit 5)
and:
...He tried calling your mum drunk on Xmas saying he’d take the baby and get on the spirit...

(Exhibit 5)
In early 2025, a Family Violence Order was made against the father which remains in force for a period of 12 months.
On 30 April 2025, the proceedings were transferred to Division 1 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia.
On 13 November 2025, the matter was set down for trial for five days commencing 2 February 2026. The hearing commenced on 2 February 2026 and concluded with submissions on 3 February 2026 with judgment reserved.

THE EVIDENCE - PARENTING

Each of the parties and the ICL filed an Outline of Case identifying the affidavits upon which they intended to rely. Both the mother and father were cross-examined, as was the single expert.
I have read all the material relied upon, including the exhibits. Whilst the evidence raised a series of issues, I am not required to address every fact or submission made (Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48; [2003] 200 ALR 447).
Given the ambit of the orders sought, the primary issues in the parenting case include the following:
(1) Whether the mother and/or the child have been exposed to or subjected to family violence by the father.

(2) Whether the child is at an unacceptable risk of harm.

(3) Does an order for supervision render the risk of harm acceptable.

(4) Whether there is a benefit to the child in spending supervised time with the father.

(5) Should an order be made for the father to receive information in relation to the child.

A Court Child Expert prepared a Child Impact Report dated 22 March 2024 (“Child Impact Report”) following interviews with the parties and observation of the mother and child (Exhibit 10).
The Court Child Expert was not required for cross examination, and I accept her observations and opinions. Relevant to the determination to be made in the child’s best interests, the Court Child Expert reported as follows:
[Ms Frost] reported a significant and detailed narrative of her experiences of frequent sexual abuse, coercive and controlling behaviour and emotional abuse during her relationship with [Mr Frost]. [Ms Frost’s] commentary surrounding her experiences suggested that she has endured years of family violence from [Mr Frost] since the commencement of their relationship, stating that she has only recent begun reflecting on the extent of her endured abuse through her therapeutic work with [Q Mental Health Services] (family violence support program). Also, by reading and understanding the judgement for [Mr Frost’s] prior family law matter in regards to his older daughter and relationship with his ex-partner. [Ms Frost] reported that [Mr Frost’s] perpetration of sexual abuse also extended towards his elder daughter during his prior relationship. [Ms Frost] recalled multiple incidents of sexual assault which were professionally confronting to assess, particularly due to the repetitive nature of assaults, and severity, with [X] being frequently exposed to the attacks.
As is often associated with sexual abuse, [Ms Frost] reported behaviour by [Mr Frost] towards her which was overtly controlling, such as monitoring her movements, controlling finances, and limiting her contact with friends and family. If found to be true, [Mr Frost’s] engagement with such behaviour, appears to be premeditated and purposeful. [Mr Frost] vehemently denies any engagement of family violence and sexual abuse towards [Ms Frost], suggesting that [Ms Frost] is lying, believing she has been influenced by his ex-partner’s allegations against him and is motivated to have sole parental responsibility for [X] following their arguments surrounding domestic duties and family relationships. [Mr Frost’s] belief for [Ms Frost’s] motivation for the current dispute appeared to be somewhat disproportionate and untenable to their current stage of parental dispute, whilst also minimising concerns towards his historical allegations of family violence and child abuse.
[Ms Frost] did not report her experiences of abuse to authorities during her relationship with [Mr Frost], rather provided information of her experiences of sexual abuse to police following their separation which resulted in the issuing of a PFVO in [...] 2023. It is not uncommon for survivors of family violence to seek support following the cessation of violent relationships rather than during, as a protective and survival technique especially in regards to more experiences of more intimate forms of family violence, which appears to be relevant for [Ms Frost]...
[Mr Frost], accepted that he has been subject to a previous family violence order, stating that it was unjustified following a verbal argument with his ex-partner. [Mr Frost] was not forthcoming in regard to his engagement with violent behaviour despite police information indicating otherwise, suggestive of limited reflective capacity. [Mr Frost’s] narrative surrounding his past behaviour is suggestive that there is a high risk of him reengaging in similar behaviour into the future. Further information surrounding the outcome of any investigation of [Mr Frost] by the sex crime unit or other department, would likely be of use in informing these proceedings.
...
[Ms Frost] reported concerns surrounding [Mr Frost’s] engagement with drug and alcohol abuse and the impact this also had on his perpetration of family violence. [Ms Frost] said that [Mr Frost] used marijuana and alcohol daily resulting in him becoming increasingly of agitated and shouting at her. [Ms Frost] also reported that [Mr Frost] has previously been assessed by a drug and alcohol counsellor, and was diagnosed with a dependent personality disorder, however there is no collateral material to indicate any diagnosis has been made. [Mr Frost] accepted that he previously used alcohol and marijuana daily during his relationship with [Ms Frost], but stated that it did not affect his mood or behaviour. [Mr Frost] accepted that he continues to consume several beers an evening currently and more on weekend, also using marijuana twice a week for pain relief. If [Mr Frost’s] diagnosis of dependent personality disorder is found to be true, it would appear that [Mr Frost] has very limited insight into his functioning and impact of his behaviour on others, especially in regards to risks towards [X] of incapacitated parenting. Drug and alcohol affected parenting often results in limited emotional availability towards children, incapacitated ability to risk assess and protect, as well as having a high intersection with increased family violence, which has been reported to be a significant issue for [Mr Frost]...
Mr and [Ms Frost] also confirmed that [Mr Frost] has been involved in early family law matters in regards to his eldest daughter, [H]. Both Mr and [Ms Frost] reported that there were allegations that [Mr Frost] sexually abused [H]. It was reported that [Mr Frost] no longer spends any time with [H] as a result of the prior proceedings, despite [Mr Frost] suggesting the outcome of such was a result of his poor legal representation, opposed to any risk towards [H] in his care. [Ms Frost] believed that the outcome of prior proceedings was the result of unmanageable risk for [H] when in [Mr Frost’s] care due to the possibility of her experiencing emotional and sexual abuse. Despite such, it appears as though [Mr Frost] has not been legally charged with any offences relating to sexual abuse of a minor...

...
[X] has not spent significant nor substantial time with [Mr Frost] since September 2023, which is a significant period of [X’s] life. [X] is of an age where she is unable to understand the reason behind [Mr Frost’s] absence, but would likely be aware of his missing presence. Children generally benefit from ongoing time with both parents following a separation if it is safe for them to be able to do so. However, given the significance of the risks associated for [X’s] time with [Mr Frost], and his denial and/or limited reflective capacity, further assessment is required to clarify if [X] spending time with [Mr Frost] is safe. Historically however, the risks appear to have been mitigated and [X] protected when spending time with [Mr Frost] in a supervised setting, such as [N Contact Centre]. In the interim, if the court views that it is safe for [X] to do so, [X] may benefit from ongoing supervised time with [Mr Frost] to maintain her connection with him, pending the possible outcome of these proceedings.

...
Whilst [Mr Frost] appears to want to have a meaningful and significant relationship with [X], he did not demonstrate capacity or willingness to want to change or challenge his existing pattern of behaviour which has been reported to be harmful towards his elder daughter and other significant relationships. This is largely problematic and adult-centric in narrative, as [Mr Frost] appears to be prioritising his own needs above that of [X’s]. [Mr Frost] may benefit from some education surrounding his proposal in alignment with [X’s] age and stage of development and the impact of being separated from her primary care giver at such a young age, as this would likely have a destabilising impact on her emotional and psychological health. Whilst [Mr Frost] either denies or struggles to reflect on any behaviour of concern, it is unlikely that any formal intervention will result in any meaningful change to his behaviour. However, were he to engage in a men's behaviour change program, this may be a positive step in evidencing his commitment to [X] and may assist in his developing his capacity for self-reflection.

...
Whilst it is understood that there is an ongoing investigation from Tasmania Police in regards to [Mr Frost’s] alleged offences, the outcome of any investigation would likely be of significance in informing the outcome of these proceedings. In the interim, given the allegations of family violence, mental health and drug use in this matter, it is suggested that any supervised time proceed with caution, on the basis that this may create risks to [Ms Frost] and [X’s] safety, and may not be able to be consistently maintained. If supervised time does proceed, it would be best placed to occur at [N Contact Centre].

(Child Impact Report dated 22 March 2024)
In evidence, and relied upon by the mother and the ICL, are the reasons for judgment of McGuire J in Frost & Morrison [2022] FedCFamC1F 1034 (“Frost & Morrison”) (Exhibit 6). On 22 December 2022, McGuire J, following a five-day hearing, made orders and delivered reasons in relation to the father’s application to spend time with his daughter from an earlier relationship.
Some of the issues in the proceedings before his Honour bore similarities with the issues before this Court. In those proceedings, the father’s former partner alleged that she had been sexually assaulted by the father. His Honour’s reasons record as follows:
The father, Mr Frost, is the applicant. In circumstances where he concedes not having spent time with [X] since May 2020, and where there are issues of alleged child sexual abuse/unacceptable risk, the father concedes that [X] should live primarily with the mother. He proposes an order for equal shared parental responsibility. He suggests a sympathetic and staged reintroduction of his relationship with [X] including, if necessary, a period of supervised time and the assistance of Family Therapy.
The mother, Ms Morrison, asks for an order for sole parental responsibility for [X]. Coupled with this she proposes a specific order changing [X’s] surname from “[Frost]” to “[Morrison]”. This is in circumstances where the mother argues that [X] has been sexually abused by the father and where there is no current relationship between father and child. The mother does not propose any orders for the time between [X] and the father.

...
The father denies all allegations that he has sexually assaulted [X]. He concedes that he has bathed naked with [X]. He concedes that he may have cleaned her genital area. The implication of the father's argument is that the mother has either manipulated the child and/or coached the child to make false allegations for her own agenda thwarting his relationship with [X].

...
The father also denies each and all of the mother’s allegations of family violence asserted to be perpetrated by him against her including allegations of sexual assault/rape.

...
The mother alleges sexual assaults on her by the father in early 2015 and specifically on [...] December 2015. The father denies both allegations.
The mother alleges sexual assault by the father on the number of occasions in early 2016 including a specific occasion in about April 2016 at the home of the paternal grandparents. The father denies the allegations.

...
The mother alleges that the father bathed naked on occasions with [X] until she was of crawling age and that she observed the father in the bath with [X] and that his penis was erect.

...
Late May 2020 [X] returned from spending time with the father and [Ms C]. The mother alleges:
[X] refused to have a bath, screamed and went red and continued to cry;
Later that night the mother alleges that [X] said “I don't really want to have a bath with dad anymore because it really hurts when he cleans my vagina”. The mother responded “what?” and [X] is alleged to have said: “I don’t like it when he puts his finger in, it hurts”. The mother alleges that [X] asked her mother not to tell anyone “as she would get into big trouble”; and
The mother alleges that [X] made further disclosures over the coming days that the father had touched her vagina when he was washing it and that he put his finger in it and it hurt.
The father denies having sexually assaulted [X] and denies inserting his finger into her vagina. The father does, however, concede bathing naked with child at around the relevant time and volunteers that he felt required to cleanse the child’s genital area.
...
In respect of the allegations of sexual abuse of [X], the father asserts that the mother has “blatantly lied” about this and says “I have never sexually abused anyone and not my daughter [X]” [82].
...
Save for his denials, the father’s trial affidavit does not deal in any detail with the allegations of sexual abuse of [X] leading to the issue of unacceptable risk raised by the mother and the ICL. That is, his affidavit is silent to details of bathing with [X] in mid-2020 such as believing the child to have an infection and a white discharge or ‘pus’ all of which were detailed to.
I had the benefit of seeing and hearing the father give evidence and be cross-examined. He was understandably nervous but presented as articulate and intelligent. He showed a self-confidence often to the stage of entitlement or empowerment in the giving of his evidence notably by his volunteered statement that he had not (for reasons that escape me) seen fit to read some of the affidavit material. My observations were of an entitlement consistent with the mother’s evidence of a power imbalance in and perhaps corroborative of family violence during the relationship.
...
On the balance of probabilities I prefer the evidence of the mother as to the history of the relationship between the parents and particularly as to the power imbalance involving aspects of violence together with the father’s early ambivalence in his relationship with [X].
I find in favour of the mother’s generally unchallenged evidence that she was proactive in encouraging more time for [X] with the father until [X’s] alleged disclosures.
...
Given the relatively high standard and onus of proof in respect of these issues pursuant to s 140 of the Evidence Act, I cannot be satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the father has sexually assaulted [X] in the particulars alleged.
...
I turn to consider the issue of unacceptable risk for [X] in the care of the father. On consideration, I find that there is such an unacceptable risk and in doing so I reference the factors set out under [282] herein. Specifically, [X] has made disclosures to three separate people. Those disclosures are highly particularised. The evidence is that the disclosures are made in age-appropriate language. The evidence of [Dr L] and [Ms V] is contrary to assertions that [X] has been coached or manipulated in her disclosures. The father’s explanation for achieving an erection whilst bathing with [X] is not supported by [Dr L’s] medical opinion. Equally, [Dr L] thinks the father’s assertion of [X] suffering genital inflammation/pus/white discharge is medically highly unlikely and where there is no corroborative evidence of the father’s assertions and where I find that he probably did not immediately advise his wife [Ms C], a health professional, of these asserted observations of [X] and perhaps not at all. It follows that I am comfortably persuaded on the balance of probabilities that the father represents a risk of sexual abuse for [X] and that such a risk is prima facie unacceptable.
The question for the Court then becomes whether such an unacceptable risk can be mitigated to “acceptable” or whether protections and constraints can be provided to alleviate the risk? The obvious tool available is one of supervision. This would allow a continuation (or re-introduction) of a father/daughter relationship during short but frequent and regular monitored visits. Indeed the father volunteered the possibility of a graduated regime which included a period of supervision.
There are, however, contrary indicators towards supervision being in the best interests of [X]. Firstly, there has been no relationship between [X] and the father since mid-2020 when she was just four years of age. The Court must therefore consider any manifest benefit to the child in such a limited regime and one constrained and defined by the presence of another person where there is no current relationship and unlikely to be any independent recall by [X] of her previous relationship with the father. Secondly, [Dr Q] recognises an innate fear and reluctance in [X] where [Dr Q] is an expert engaged to assist the Court in its determination by her experience and expertise. Whilst I am not bound by [Dr Q’s] opinions and recommendations, I am equally able to be assisted by and adopt her opinions. Again, [Dr Q] withstood cross-examination and was an impressive professional who was firmly of the view that [X’s] emotional health would be compromised by even supervised time. I accept her evidence and conclusions. I am not persuaded therefore that supervised time is in [X’s] best interests
...
The ambiguity in the evidence of the father and his wife, [Ms C], as to whether the father advised [Ms C] of what he says was an inflamed/pus genital area continues to concern the Court. Further, the father’s general sense of entitlement observed in the witness box and as identified, for example, by his failure to even read much of the affidavit material in this matter causes concerns as to his insight and understanding of the Court process and the concerns raised in respect of his daughter and hence his willingness to comply with court orders with which he might not agree.
...
Finally, if [X] is to live with the mother and have no contact with father and where the relationship between the parents is non-communicative, non-cooperative, and suspicious, I am satisfied that the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility and s 61DA of the Act is rebutted (if not rendered inapplicable by reasons of family violence). Consequently, the mother should have sole parental responsibility for [X] provided she keep the father advised of any serious medical issues in respect of [X]
His Honour made final orders that the child was to spend no face-to-face time with the father.
Prepared for the hearing was a Single Expert Report by Dr Ratcliff, psychiatrist, which became Exhibit 11. In his report, Dr Ratcliff recorded the following in relation to the father:
...His spontaneous history is notable in that he attempted no explanation of his predicament. His narrative was egocentric with no apparent search for or comprehension of context or the motivations of others. He mentioned what he did, expressed fleeting regret at the outcomes, made statements about what the woman did as if that was to be expected, and without indignation. There appeared to be a background sense of entitlement but this was not emphasised in interview. It appeared in affidavit material with mention of child support in relation to contact with [H]. The relationship with [Ms Morrison] is found to be unsatisfactory, attributed simplistically to her jealousy of his other contacts and liaisons, and [Ms Frost’s] attractiveness. He felt entitled to a new relationship, it developed rapidly, led to marriage and pregnancy. He made [Ms Morrison’s] statements known to [Ms Frost] and was mildly affected by her (perhaps unsurprising) part-belief in them. He attributed conflict with [Ms Frost] to disagreements about dominance in the relationship. He categorically denied rape in any relationship, claiming "normal sex, nothing weird'; but he does not contest contacting telephone pornography, dating sites, and casual liaisons are mentioned as if they are normal entitlements like intramarital sexual relationships. Cannabis and alcohol are regarded as normal accompaniments to life...

(Single Expert Report dated 17 July 2025, p.7–8)
In relation to the hospital records concerning the child’s birth (Exhibit 2), Dr Ratcliff opined as follows:
The records provide clear professional observational evidence of coercive control and [Ms Frost’s] compliance with this. From [Mr Frost’s] history his experience of the pain of a fracture was when he was aged fifteen in about 2003.

(Single Expert Report dated 17 July 2025, p.13)
In relation to the father’s driving record, Dr Ratcliff opined as follows:
...The majority might be regarded as offences of irresponsibility combined with an impression of regarding the law as inapplicable to the offender, consistent with a sense of entitlement.

(Single Expert Report dated 17 July 2025, p.17)
In relation to the father, Dr Ratcliff opined as follows:
...At any stage of his life, any female partner he may have can expect the relationship to be paralleled and eventually replaced by another. His attitude to women appears to be that they should be sexually attractive, available and compliant. An ability to attract, to please and eventually to establish a sexual relationship with a suitable woman can be viewed as one face of the need to control. The more negative face of that would emerge later in reported controlling behaviour.

[Mr Frost’s] likely attitude to daughters in early childhood is an essence of the present case. He had claimed a close and normal relationship with his daughter [H] that continued during agreed contact after he had abandoned the mother [Ms Morrison]. After the child was withheld from him he sought to re-establish contact with one pretext being his fidelity in providing financial child support Allowing for the natural interest and fascination of a parent for a young child there is a nuance of a view of the child as a possession, and in view of other evidence of a tendency to control and subordinate females this requires consideration.

...

The Father, [Mr Frost] displays behavioural and personality characteristics consistent with disorders resembling 1) Compulsive Sexual Behaviour Disorder (according to ICD-11), 2) Cannabis Use Disorder (according to DSM-5 TR) but not satisfying a sufficiency of clinical features to establish these diagnoses.

...

In view of his acknowledged proclivities, his past behaviour, his use of cannabis and alcohol, and his insistence on his innocence in relation to allegations of sexual behaviour towards a child, and sexual abuse of female partners, he cannot be regarded as a safe unsupervised carer for [X].

(Single Expert Report dated 17 July 2025, p.20–22, 25)
Exhibit 9 in these proceedings are documents produced by Tasmania Police pursuant to s 67ZBE of the Act. Those documents record the following:
[...] – [...]/01/ 2025

Nil children recorded as present.

This incident relates to [Mr Frost] attempting to video call Party A, in breach of FVO conditions. Police attended [Mr Frost’s] address and advised him he was under arrest to which he immediately stated “No, I won’t be going anywhere” and walked away from police. Police again advised [Mr Frost] that he was under arrest and held onto his arm. [Mr Frost] stated, “Don’t touch me” and began resisting the arrest by not allowing police to place handcuffs on him and by pulling his arms to the front of his body. [Mr Frost] then shrugged his arms, freeing himself of police grip, and commenced running away from them. When police went to grab [Mr Frost], he turned and shoved an officer to the chest with both arms. [Mr Frost] was warned by police that OC spray would be utilised given the resistance and noncompliance displayed by him. [Mr Frost] again attempted to run from police but was caught a short distance away.

[Mr Frost] disobeyed multiple police instructions to stop resisting and place his hands behind his back. He then struck a police officer to the face and reached for his police issued firearm. A struggle on the ground ensued for approximately four minutes with [Mr Frost] continually refusing to put his hands behind his back.

Outcome: [Mr Frost] was charged with breach of an order and other numerous offences.

...

[Mr Frost] has the following matters currently pending:
- Assault police officer, Breach of bail conditions, Breach of Family Violence Order, Resist a police officer – alleged offence dates being [...] 2025 and [...] 2025 (next appearance for mention on [...] 2025, not guilty plea entered)

- Common assault x2 (complainant Party A), Destroy property, Possess controlled plant or its products, Cultivating controlled plant – alleged offence date being [...] 2024, [...] 2024, and [...] 2025 (next appearance for mention on [...]2025, not guilty plea entered)

- Contravene conditions of a notice, Use abusive language to police officer – alleged offence dates being [...] 2024 and [...] 2024 (next appearance for mention on [...] 2025, not guilty plea entered)

- Rape [...], Attempted rape (complainant Party A) – alleged offence dates being between [...] 2023 and [...] 2024 (next appearance for plea on [...] 2025)

- Rape [...] (complainant [Ms Frost]) – alleged offence dates being between [...] 2019 and [...] 2022 (next appearance for plea on [...] 2025)

- Rape [...] (complainant Party B) – alleged offence dates being between [...] 2015 and [...] 2017 (next appearance for plea on [...] 2025)

...
[...] – January 2025

In October 2024, [Mr Frost] attended the police station in relation to the rape charges against him. [Mr Frost] was noted to be erratic in his behaviour and his mood was very up and down, changing often. He appeared to show a dislike for one officer and stated, “Your time will come”. When asked if this was a threat, [Mr Frost] said “You’ll see”.

In January 2025, information was received that [Mr Frost] was attempting to find out the address of the above police officer. Further, the reporting person advised that [Mr Frost] has an explosive temper, drinks heavily, and is a heavy cannabis user.

Outcome: Filed for intelligence only.

[...] – January 2025

Police attended [Mr Frost’s] residence in relation to family violence offences. During a search of the residence, police located a six cannabis plants being cultivated.

Outcome: [Mr Frost] was charged with drug related offences.

(As per the original)

APPLICABLE LAW- PARENTING

Parenting proceedings are governed by Pt VII of the Act. In making a parenting order, s 60CA of the Act requires the Court to regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration. In determining what is in the child’s best interests, s 60CC(1) of the Act provides the Court must consider the matters set out in s 60CC(2) of the Act. The matters to be considered are as follows:
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;
(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

In addressing the matters in s 60CC(2A) of the Act, the Court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child together with any family violence order that applies in relation to the child or a member of the child’s family.
Section 60CG of the Act requires the Court, when making a parenting order, to ensure the order does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence and is consistent with an existing family violence order.
Family violence, in light of the statutory considerations, is a matter relevant to the making of a parenting order. The answer to a question of future risk can be informed by anterior factual findings, including findings of family violence. In that respect, the Full Court in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 observed as follows:
Long ago, in parenting proceedings characterised by one parent’s allegation that the other had sexually abused their child, the High Court of Australia (“the High Court”) emphasised the distinction between two very different things: on the one hand, proving alleged sexual abuse according to the civil standard of proof and, on the other, establishing the risk of the feared sexual abuse occurring in the future: (M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69).
In respect of the first issue, the High Court said (at 76):
In considering an allegation of sexual abuse, the court should not make a positive finding that the allegation is true unless the court is so satisfied according to the civil standard of proof, with due regard to the factors mentioned in Briginshaw v. Briginshaw.

(Footnote omitted)
...
In relation to the second question pertaining to risk, the High Court said (at 77–78):
... [T]he court must determine whether on the evidence there is a risk of sexual abuse occurring if custody or access be granted and assess the magnitude of that risk. ... [T]he test is best expressed by saying that a court will not grant custody or access to a parent if that custody or access would expose the child to an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse.
...
M v M put beyond doubt the proposition that courts exercising jurisdiction under Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) must protect children from credible risks of harm due to sexual abuse. Such risks, like all prospective events, are capable of classification in only one of three mutually exclusive categories: possibilities, probabilities, or certainties. Once it is accepted courts should (and do) react to dangers in the form of risks of harm which may merely be possibilities, it is an oxymoron to expect such possibilities to then be forensically proven on the balance of probabilities according to the civil standard of proof. By definition, possibilities are not, and could never be, probabilities. Risks of harm are not susceptible of scientific demonstration or proof (CDJ v VAJ (1998) 197 CLR 172 at [151]), but are instead postulated from known historical facts and present circumstances.

(As per the original)
In relation to the question of unacceptable risk, Tree J succinctly summarised the risk assessment in Chiles & Petrenko (2024) FLC 94-197 in the following terms:
...It bears repeating that the usual course in undertaking the assessment is to identify the contended risk event, gauge the likelihood of it occurring, and then consider the magnitude of harm which might ensue if the risk event does occur. One then considers whether in the light of those matters the risk of harm is acceptable or not.
The purpose of ameliorative conditions is to mitigate a risk of harm from unacceptable to acceptable, such that when the risk analysis is repeated with the ameliorative condition in place, the relevant risk is assessed as acceptable.
The issue of risk and whether it is unacceptable will be addressed below.

DISCUSSION- PARENTING

The mother contends that she has been the victim of family violence, and her counsel urges the Court to make a finding of unacceptable risk, submitting that the risk of harm cannot be ameliorated by the imposition of supervision as proposed by the father.
The mother’s affidavit as to family violence paints a shocking and disturbing picture of a young woman being subjected to, and exposed to, degrading and abusive incidents of family violence, some of which occurred in the presence of the child. The Court Child Expert described the mother’s recounting of the sexual assaults incidents as “professionally confronting to assess, particularly due to the repetitive nature of assaults, and severity, with [X] being frequently exposed to the attacks” (Child Impact Report, paragraph 12), a view which I can well accept.
The mother gives evidence of being subjected to incidents of family violence perpetrated by the father that included being vaginally penetrated when she was asleep (mother’s affidavit, paragraphs 74 and 82), forced to engage in oral sex (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 86), being groped (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 79) and being verbally abused (mother’s affidavit, paragraphs 70, 97 and 98). She gives compelling and persuasive evidence that the father conducted himself with a sense of entitlement, such that when she refused his sexual demands, she was abused (mother’s affidavit, paragraphs 81, 87, 93 and 94). The mother’s evidence further deposed to conduct by the father designed to coerce and control her (mother’s affidavit, paragraphs 115, 144, 145 and 146). Following the parties’ separation, she describes being harassed by him, including attending at her home unannounced (mother’s affidavit, paragraphs 125 and 126).
The mother gives evidence of the consequences of the family violence she suffered, as follows:
The family violence I experienced from [Mr Frost] has caused me to have lasting psychological impacts and for example, I am fearful every time I see a car matching the description of his. I find myself checking vehicles every time I walk outside or drive places and the sight of any [vehicle] like his makes my stomach sink. My heart rate increases, my mouth goes dry, and I start looking for somewhere to hide or run. I believe this to be a 'flight/fight response
I have also limited my social circle significantly to include only those people that I trust, and I do not like to meet new people.
I am 38 years old and I wanted to have a family with more children but I do not consider that I will be able to have more children now, I simply don't think I will be able to have a relationship again.
...
I do not think I would cope if [Mr Frost] were to spend any time with [X] for fear that she would be harmed by him or exposed to his violence towards others and to his drug and alcohol lifestyle which appears to have increased since our separation.

...
[Mr Frost] has not been aware of where [X] and I live since 5 weeks post separation. I am fearful that [Mr Frost] will somehow find out where [X] and I are living. I am concerned that he would stalk and harm me and/or [X] if he finds out.

...
The thought of [Mr Frost] spending any time with [X] is unbearable for me.
I genuinely believe that [X] would be at significant risk of physical, psychological and emotional harm if she spent any time with [Mr Frost] due to his abusive, unsafe behaviour.

(Mother’s affidavit filed 6 November 2025)
I found the mother to be a convincing and truthful witness. Her evidence was rich in detail in relation to the incidents of family violence she suffered at the hands of the father. No aspect of her evidence was impeached in cross-examination.
Her allegations of controlling behaviour are supported by the hospital records from the time of the child’s birth (Exhibit 8), which Dr Ratcliff described as “professional observational evidence of coercive control” (Single Expert Report, p.13). Dr Ratcliff opined that the father had a “tendency to control and subordinate females” (Single Expert Report, p.21). Dr Ratcliff was not cross-examined on these opinions, which I accept.
The mother gives detailed evidence of the father’s threats towards her, as well as his anger and aggression. There is also evidence of the father acting aggressively to police, assaulting police officers and threatening them.
Remarkably, but perhaps consistent with the father’s presentation before McGuire J (when facing allegations of sexual assault of a child and of that child’s mother), the father offered no more than a blanket denial. He elected not to comply with directions to file his affidavit, and the only written evidence he adduced in response to the extensive history of family violence was to state that:
I did not inflict family violence (and/or sexual violence) upon [Ms Frost]. I am not a risk to [X].
(Father’s affidavit filed 22 March 2024)
The father faces very serious criminal charges which, according to Exhibit 9 include multiple counts of rape and attempted rape. Three of the rape counts cover the period between February 2015 and November 2017, several rape counts cover the period between February 2019 and July 2022 and several more attempted rape counts cover the period between July 2023 and December 2024. If ultimately established at trial, this would constitute a period of offending spanning nine years. In addition to charges involving the sexual assaults of women, he faces charges of breaching bail, common assault and using abusive language. The father failed to comply with disclosure obligations by not providing any evidence as to the status of the criminal proceedings before the courts, including the Supreme Court of Tasmania. His obligation included disclosure of the precise charges he faced, the stage the proceedings had reached, whether he had entered a plea and, if so, when, the bail conditions imposed and the date fixed for final hearing.
I find the father to be an unconvincing and unreliable witness and prefer the evidence of the mother where it conflicts with his. In coming to this conclusion, I recognise that these are civil proceedings and that the standard of proof is governed by s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), namely proof on the balance of probabilities. In reaching my findings, I have taken into account all of the evidence, including the gravity of the matters alleged by the mother against the father. I reach this conclusion for the reasons set out above and below.
In cross-examination, despite having previously sworn to the truth of his affidavit, the father said that a paragraph of his affidavit was untrue, thereby immediately calling into question his veracity. His cross-examination was at times characterised by an argumentative disposition and deliberately non-responsive answers, which I am satisfied he adopted to avoid answering questions he found difficult. At other times, he adopted an air of insouciance which was neither convincing nor persuasive.
Dr Ratcliff described the father as having a sense of entitlement. Justice McGuire similarly described the father as having “an entitlement consistent with the mother’s evidence of a power imbalance in and perhaps corroborative of family violence during the relationship” (Frost & Morrison at [133]). I accept the observations of Dr Ratcliff and his Honour, which accord with my own assessment of the evidence.
In circumstances where the mother’s evidence of family violence was not the subject of any cross-examination, where I find the father to be an unconvincing witness, and where I find the mother a convincing and truthful witness, it leads me to prefer the mother’s evidence to that of the father. The consequence of that finding is that I accept the mother’s evidence, and I am satisfied that incidents of family violence occurred during the relationship, perpetrated by the father against the mother as asserted in her affidavit.
I accept the unchallenged evidence of the Court Child Expert. In referencing the mother’s allegations of family violence and coercive and controlling behaviour, she stated that “if found to be true, [Mr Frost’s] engagement with such behaviour, appears to be premeditated and purposeful” (Child Impact Report, paragraph 13). She later opined that the father’s narrative “surrounding his past behaviour is suggestive that there is a high risk of him reengaging in similar behaviour into the future” (Child Impact Report, paragraph 15).
Taking all the above matters into account, I am comfortably satisfied that the father poses a danger to the mother and to his children such that he poses an unacceptable risk of harm. Dr Ratcliff expressed the matter in the following terms: “in view of his acknowledged proclivities, his past behaviour, his use of cannabis and alcohol, and his insistence on his innocence in relation to allegations of sexual behaviour towards a child, and sexual abuse of female partners, he cannot be regarded as a safe unsupervised carer for [X]” (Single Expert Report, p.25).
I find, consistent with the observations of the Court Child Expert, that the evidence establishes the father has very limited insight into the impact of his behaviours on others. The father proposes that his time with the child occur in a supervised setting and submits that supervision would reduce the risk from unacceptable to acceptable. I do not accept that submission.
Although the father proposes spending supervised time, he has adduced no evidence that a “Child Support Facility” is available to facilitate the time arrangement he seeks, particularly in light of the very serious criminal charges he faces.
The child has had no relationship with the father for over three years. In cross-examination, Dr Ratcliff said the father would be “a stranger” to the child. The proposed supervised time would therefore occur in an unfamiliar environment, in the presence of strangers, and with a person who is, to the child, a stranger. The father faces very serious criminal charges which, if he were convicted, could result in a term of imprisonment for many years, thereby creating the real prospect of a further interruption to any relationship between the child and the father.
In answer to questions from the ICL regarding the impact on the child if time were to resume but then cease again if the father was imprisoned, Dr Ratcliff said the child would not be distressed but would be perplexed. I am not persuaded by Dr Ratcliff’s opinion that the child would be merely perplexed by such a disruption. Dr Ratcliff’s opinion can only be expressed in the abstract, where he has not seen or interviewed the child. Further, no aspect of Dr Ratcliff’s evidence explored the potential benefit to the child of such a relationship, which cannot simply be presumed.
The Court Child Expert observed in her report, although the father “appears to want to have a meaningful and significant relationship with [X], he did not demonstrate capacity or willingness to want to change or challenge his existing pattern of behaviour which has been reported to be harmful towards his elder daughter and other significant relationships” (Child Impact Report, paragraph 23).
The father’s past conduct towards women and children demonstrates his dangerousness and that his child is unsafe in his presence. I am satisfied that his parenting capacity is consequently compromised and impaired. I am not the first judicial officer to reach such a conclusion, noting McGuire J, following a contested hearing, determined the father posed such an unacceptable risk of harm to another child that he should have no time with that child (Frost & Morrison).
The father’s conduct towards the mother was appalling, and his attitude towards women is disgraceful. Consequently, he presents a poor role model for the child. I am not persuaded that even supervised time would be in the child’s best interests.
I address below those factors pursuant to s 60CC of the Act that are relevant to my determination and have not already been addressed.

s 60CC(2)(a) – what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:

(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child)

I am satisfied, as a consequence of my acceptance of the mother’s evidence and the previous findings of another court, that the father poses an unacceptable risk of harm. I am further satisfied that the arrangements proposed by the father, which would require the mother to attend at a contact centre to facilitate time with the child, would not promote the mother’s safety in that they would cause her to be fearful.
The only arrangements that promote the safety of both the mother and the child are those proposed by the mother and the ICL.

s 60CC(2)(b) – any views expressed by the child

The child has not spent any time with the father since September 2023, at which stage the child was two years old. The child is now five years old and there is no evidence of her views and even if there were, her age is such that no weight could be ascribed to them.

s 60CC(2)(c) – the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child

The child is five years old and is entirely dependent upon the mother for all her needs.
In his Case Outline, the father submitted that the child requires protection, stability and certainty. I am satisfied that the father’s proposals are the antithesis of protection, stability and certainty.
For the reasons given, I am not persuaded that supervised time as proposed by the father would be protective of the child. I am satisfied that, given the possibility of the father’s conviction for serious criminal offences and the prospects of imprisonment, such an outcome would create both uncertainty and instability for the child.

s 60CC(2)(d) – the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

There is no dispute that the mother can provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs. In his Case Outline, the father acknowledged that the mother has the capacity to meet the child’s needs.
That submission is reinforced by the terms in the Child Impact Report, and the observations of the Court Child Expert regarding the mother’s parenting capacity, and the father’s concession that the mother should have sole decision-making responsibility.

s 60CC(2)(e) – the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents and other people who are significant to the child where it is safe to do so

The evidence of family violence, the findings of McGuire J and my findings and reasons set out above are such that I am comfortably satisfied that the child would be at an unacceptable risk of harm in the event the child was to spend unsupervised time with the father. Whilst the father seeks supervised time, I am not persuaded that such an arrangement moves the risk of harm to one that is acceptable.

s 60CC(2)(f) – anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the case

There is no other matter that is a relevant consideration.

DISPOSITION

For the reasons outlined above, I am satisfied that it is in the child’s best interest to spend no time with the father.
The father seeks to be provided with information in relation to the child’s development, progress at school and her health and general wellbeing. Any benefit from such information would be solely for the father; there is no evidence that it would benefit the child. Accordingly, no such order will not be made.
I propose to make the orders as sought by the mother.

FINANCIAL PROCEEDINGS

The approach to be adopted in a financial adjustment case under s 79 of the Act is to follow the well-recognised four-step process (see Hickey and Hickey and Attorney-General for the Commonwealth of Australia (Intervener) [2003] FamCA 395; (2003) FLC 93-143). Following such an approach, the Court identifies and values the existing assets and liabilities at the date of hearing for the purposes of division. Secondly, the Court assesses the contributions of the parties within the meaning of s 79(4) of the Act. Thirdly, the Court identifies the relevant matters under s 79(5) and determines such adjustment as is necessary to the contribution assessment. Finally, the Court considers the effect of the findings and determines whether the order as proposed is, in all the circumstances, just and equitable.
Whilst no submission was made consistent with the ratio arising out of the High Court’s determination in Stanford v Stanford [2012] FamCAFC 1; (2012) 247 CLR 108, I am of the view that it is just and equitable that an order be made adjusting the property interests of the parties. The parties are no longer living together and there is no longer any common use of their property. The assumptions and undertakings that governed the use of their property ended with separation, and both parties sought that there be an adjustive order.
Section 79(3)(a) of the Act, in instructive language directs the Court, in considering what order (if any) should be made under s 79, to identify:
(i) The existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(ii) The existing liabilities of the parties to the marriage or either of them.

The parties were agreed as to the composition of the asset pool, save in relation to the value to be ascribed to the former matrimonial home. The property had been valued by Ms G, a real estate valuer jointly engaged by the parties, on 9 May 2024 in the sum of $575,000 (Exhibit 12).
The final orders sought by the parties differed in respect of that property. The mother sought an order for the sale of the former matrimonial home, whereas the father sought to retain it and pay an amount to the mother. The mother had earlier brought an application for the property to be revalued, given the age of the existing valuation. That application was opposed by the father. The mother’s application was refused, and reasons given for same in Frost & Frost [2026] FedCFamC1F 3.
As the mother put in issue the current value of the property, Ms G was required for cross-examination. In cross examination, Ms G agreed that the value as at today would not be $575,000 and that it would be higher. No attempt was made to have Ms G give an opinion as to the property’s current value.
In Taylor & Taylor & Taylor [2000] FamCA 308 at [22], the Full Court affirmed the view expressed by an earlier Full Court that the determination of a matter pursuant to s 79 of the Act first “involves a determination of ... the nature and, so far as possible, value of the property of the parties in issue in the proceedings (Pastrikos and Pastrikos [1979] FamCA 56; (1980) FLC 90-897 at 75,653).”
In circumstances where the current value of the property is not known, but is accepted to be higher than that opined nearly two years ago, the requirement to make orders that are just and equitable cannot be achieved by permitting the father to retain a property of unknown value, particularly where it comprises the single largest asset of the parties. The inevitable consequence of the oral evidence of Ms G, which I accept, is that the property will be sold and the net proceeds divided in accordance with the ultimate findings, after consideration of the matters under s 79(4) and s 79(5) of the Act (Smith & Smith (1991) FLC 92-261).
The remaining assets of the parties comprise the father’s savings of $56,000; the mother’s savings of $6,889; a motor vehicle in the mother’s possession agreed at $10,000; motor vehicles in the father’s possession agreed at $10,000; tools in the father’s possession with an agreed value of $5,000; the mother’s superannuation entitlements valued at $127,968 and the father’s superannuation entitlements valued at $9,240. Thus, the pool of property available for division between the parties, excluding the former matrimonial home, is $225,097.
Of that sum, the mother holds property to the value of $144,857 and the father holds property to the value of $80,240. The parties continue to own jointly the former matrimonial home.
The determination in financial proceedings calls for the exercise of discretion, and a holistic evaluative judgment of the respective contributions of the parties as identified in s 79(4) of the Act. Despite amendments to the Act, the observations of the Full Court in cases such as Dickons v Dickons [2012] FamCAFC 154; (2012) 50 Fam LR 244 remain apposite to the assessment of the considerations pursuant to s 79(4) of the Act. Their Honours in that case observed:
... the task of assessing contributions is holistic and but part of a yet further holistic determination of what orders, if any, represent justice and equity in the particular circumstances of this particular relationship. So much is clear from the terms of s 79 itself and, in particular, s 79(2). The essential task is to assess the nature, form and extent of the contributions of all types made by each of the parties within the context of an analysis of their particular relationship.
Doing so is also consistent with the demands of authority that the ultimate assessment of contributions should be made without “giving overzealous attention to the ascertainment of the parties’ contributions” (Norbis v Norbis [1986] HCA 17; (1986) 161 CLR 513 at 524 ; [1986] HCA 17; 65 ALR 12 at 18 ; [1986] HCA 17; 10 Fam LR 819 at 825 ; [1986] HCA 17) and the well-established recognition in the authorities (acknowledged specifically by her Honour in this case) that the process required of the court by s 79 is the exercise of a wide discretion, not the performance of a mathematical or accounting exercise.
The necessarily imprecise “wide discretion” inherent in what is required by the section is made no more precise or coherent by attributing percentage figures to arbitrary time frames or categorisations of contributions within the relationship. Indeed, we consider that doing so is contrary to the holistic analysis required by the section and, in the usual course of events, should be avoided.
In assessing the contributions of the parties, I note that the parties are agreed that they commenced cohabitation in May 2019; were married in late 2019; and separated in October 2022. Thus, the period of cohabitation was about three and a half years. The parties have been separated for almost as long as they cohabitated.
As indicated earlier, there is one child of their relationship, who was born more than a year before the parties separated.
Given the manner in which the parties drafted their affidavits, it is difficult to determine with precision the property each held at the commencement of cohabitation. Doing the best I can on the evidence available, it appears that the mother had savings of approximately $100,000, which she contributed to the purchase of the former matrimonial home in late 2019 and superannuation which, it was agreed, had a value as at 30 June 2019 of $86,686.02.
In the case of the father, he owned a property at Town K which was sold in March 2020 from which he received $182,314 (father’s affidavit, paragraph 29). He also had savings of approximately $100,000 (father’s affidavit, paragraph 15), superannuation of $25,000 (father’s affidavit, paragraph 16), two motor vehicles, a motorbike and tools, all of unknown value.
In late 2019, the parties purchased the former matrimonial home at Suburb F. The purchase price was some $420,000. The father was cross-examined about his evidence at paragraph 27 of his affidavit concerning the parties’ respective contributions to the purchase and said that the paragraph was “untrue”.
In light of his response that the evidence in that paragraph was untrue, I accept the evidence of the mother that the parties’ each contributed $100,000 from their separate savings towards the purchase of the property and they obtained a loan of $250,000.
The mother gives limited evidence of her non-financial contributions. She does say, however, that she was the sole carer for the child throughout the relationship as well as responsible for all cooking, cleaning, grocery shopping, payment of bills and other household chores. The father said that the parties shared the household work roughly equally, but that the mother did more of the cooking and he did more cleaning. He conceded that the mother undertook the greater share of the care for the child but said that he also assisted in that respect. Neither party was asked any questions on this aspect of their contributions.
The mother’s counsel indicated that there was no challenge to the contributions identified by the father at paragraph 30 of his affidavit concerning the parties’ respective financial contributions after the purchase of the home and until the mortgage was discharged. Accepting that evidence, reveals that following the purchase the mother paid $14,430 and the father paid $237,000.
The father gave detailed evidence at paragraph 32 to 36 of his affidavit as to his contributions by way of improvements to the property after its purchase. This evidence was not challenged. During the parties’ relationship, the father also made payments of child support for his child from a previous relationship.
The parties separated in October 2022, when the mother left the former matrimonial home. The father retained occupation of the home and has, at times, rented it out (as conceded by him to Dr Ratcliff) and retained the rental income which has not been disclosed or shared.
Following separation, the mother has undertaken all of the parenting responsibilities. I accept the mother’s evidence, which was not challenged, that the father’s time with the child comprised approximately eight occasions between October 2022 and February 2023 supervised by the mother, and thereafter once per week for an hour at a supervision centre between March 2023 and September 2023. The father’s financial contribution to the support of the child has been approximately $30 to $34 per week by way of child support.
In assessing the contributions, I have had regard to my earlier findings as to family violence. The mother was subjected to demeaning, degrading and humiliating incidents of family violence. This included verbal, emotional, physical and sexual abuse, as well as financial control, with elements of retribution and punishment if the father was displeased with her or if she did not do what he asked.
I am satisfied that there is both direct evidence of the impact of the family violence on the mother’s contributions and evidence from which I can infer that those incidents of family violence have affected her ability to make the contributions she did. I am comfortably satisfied that a parent who makes contributions in an environment where their partner is intimidating, aggressive and abusive, and where they are in fear of their reaction, effects the ability of that person to make contributions, in this instance as a homemaker and parent during the relationship and on her parenting contributions post separation.
In Norris v Brooks [2022] NSWSC 804, Robb J observed as follows:
A judge hearing a case like these family law proceedings should have the emotional empathy necessary to make a reasonable assessment of the subjective effect of family violence, where the effect of that violence is not as obvious as, say, the result of debilitating physical injury. The work of caring for and nurturing the physical and emotional needs of children and the general maintenance of a household may be made significantly more adverse if it has to be undertaken in a state of fear, depression, uncertainty or self-doubt. So much is a matter of general human understanding.
I respectfully agree with his Honour’s observations.
For the reasons given earlier, I am satisfied that the mother has established that the father perpetrated family violence during the relationship, which I accept affected her ability to make the pre seperation contributions that she made.
The parties cohabited for approximately three and a half years during which time they had one child. I accept that the father’s contributions, in a financial sense, exceeded those of the mother, but nevertheless acknowledged that she also made a substantial contribution to the purchase of the home. I also accept the father’s evidence that he has made substantial contributions towards the ongoing maintenance and improvement of the property. I also take into account that the asset pool includes the mother’s superannuation, which was valued at approximately $86,000 at or about the commencement of cohabitation and is now valued at approximately $127,000 and to which the father made no direct financial contribution.
I also take into account the significant contributions made by the mother during the relationship in caring for the child, as well as her overwhelming parenting contributions in the period following separation and take into account the effect of the family violence on the mother’s post separation contributions.
I am satisfied that a holistic assessment of the parties’ contributions over the course of the relationship and thereafter to the date of hearing would not accord with either the father’s or the mother’s assessment as to the contributions. I am satisfied that the mother’s assessment of 60 per cent fails to give sufficient regard to the father’s contributions. Likewise, I am satisfied that the father’s proposed assessment of 30 per cent in favour of the mother fails to adequately recognise her contributions, including her significant post-separation parenting contributions. Assessing the contributions holistically from the date of cohabitation to the date of hearing compels a finding of equality of contribution.
Addressing the matters under s 79(5), I have had regard to the age of each of the parties, the age of the child, the fact that the mother will be the sole physical carer of the child, that, given the history of child support payments to date, she is unlikely to ever receive a sum that would adequately assist her in financially supporting the child, and the limitations on her earning capacity. I also take into account the mother’s evidence as to the financial impact upon her of having the care of the child, about which she was not challenged where she states as follows:
While I am a [Health Professional], I will have to go to the expense of putting [X] in daycare and I cannot go back to work full-time because full-time work requires [people in my profession] to agree to do shift work, early mornings, evenings and nights, so I can only work casually for daytime shifts paid at lower rates. I will also be responsible for [X’s] schooling when she is old enough to attend.
There is no childcare available for shift workers as far as I am aware. Shift work attracts penalty rates giving higher earnings and it is a requirement of [my work] to be available for all three shifts.
(Mother’s affidavit filed 6 November 2025)
I also have considered the mother’s evidence as to the effect of the family violence to which she was subjected on her current and future circumstances. In that regard, I do not accept the submission of the father’s lawyer that little or no weight should be given to the mother’s evidence at paragraphs 16 and 17 of her affidavit. I accept her evidence, which was not challenged, and where I find her a convincing and truthful witness. Accordingly, I make finding findings in accordance with her evidence, where she asserts as follows:
The family violence I experienced from [Mr Frost] has caused me to have lasting psychological impacts and for example, I am fearful every time I see a car matching the description of his. I find myself checking vehicles every time I walk outside or drive places and the sight of any [vehicle] like his makes my stomach sink. My heart rate increases, my mouth goes dry, and I start looking for somewhere to hide or run. I believe this to be a 'flight/fight response.
I have also limited my social circle significantly to include only those people that I trust, and I do not like to meet new people.
(Mother’s affidavit filed 6 November 2025)
The evidence reveals that the mother’s current and future circumstances continue, in a very real and tangible way, to be affected by her experience as a victim of family violence. I also take into account the modest pool of assets. Both parties’ legal representatives contended that an adjustment in favour of the mother was appropriate. The father submitted that the adjustment should be 5 per cent, whilst the mother contended it should be 10 per cent. I am satisfied that a proper assessment of the matters under s 79(5) calls for an adjustment of 10 per cent in her favour.
I am satisfied that, having regard to the matters under s 79(4) and s 79(5), a division of the parties’ assets of 60 per cent to the mother represents a just and equitable outcome.
To give effect to this conclusion, the former matrimonial home will be sold and the proceeds divided, with 60 per cent to the mother and the balance to the father.
The remaining assets of the parties total $225,097, of which 60 per cent to the mother totals $135,058. As she holds property valued at $144,857, to give effect to the Court’s determination she will be required to pay the father, from her 60 per cent share of the proceeds of sale, the sum of $9,799.
Having regard to the mathematical effect of the division, I am satisfied that it constitutes a just and equitable determination.
In circumstances where the mother’s proposed orders for the sale of the property were not objected to, I propose to largely adopt her orders.
